speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: He was so interested in the Palmer penmanship, the Palmer system, the graceful curves and swings. He was always practicing penmanship. He found that FC didn't make as graceful a signature as it would have if it had been CF Thomas. So he legally had his name changed from Franklin Charlie to Charlie Franklin so he could have a more impressive signature. He was a beautiful writer. His penmanship was beautiful. I wish he had some of it with her, but she didn't have it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he taught school at Princeton. He probably gave the kids some good training in penmanship too.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh yes, he was teaching penmanship. That's primarily why he was interested in it, I suppose. Because we were speaking of the copy books we used to have and how our teacher would write the top line in our copy book and then we'd do our best to copy it. So because of that system, I think I have changed my penmanship every time I have changed teachers. Nobody would be able to recognize my penmanship anymore. That is not through the years. I can see it in an old cookbook that I started years and years ago. The recipes from time to time are altogether different. That is the writing. But, um, Chalice is a very nice penmanship writer too. She taught school for seven years before she moved to Aberdeen, that is in this part of the county.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Could I ask you, you mentioned when I stopped by that you'd lived a number of places before you even got to this area. Before we came to Princeton.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did that happen that you were moving around? Where did you go when you were a child?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I was born in Lee County, Iowa at Fort Madison and my dad was a locomotive engineer. And of course he would be transferred from time to time. He worked for different railroads. And then he was with the Atchison Topeka and Santa Fe and the Burlington. And so we moved from Iowa to Missouri and then to New Mexico and then to Colorado. And then for twenty years that went on, our moving about from place to place. My mother's home was in Illinois and she had always wanted my dad to be a farmer. She had come from a farm in Illinois. Nauvoo was her town. And so he put his savings into a little canvas bag that Mother made for him. Whenever he had enough he'd put a twenty dollar gold piece in that little bag. And by and by he had two bags. They were about six inches high. He had them in the back of an old clock that had been given to them for a wedding present. And I was not yet four years old but I can remember climbing up on the table beside him. He pulled down all the curtains in the dining room and then he'd get down the clock and sit on the table and take out these two little bags and put in another twenty dollar gold piece. And I was fascinated by that. Of course I didn't know what it was all about. But I learned later. And when he had two thousand dollars in twenty dollar gold pieces he bought a little farm in Nebraska. My mother had a brother who had taken up a homestead in Nebraska years before. He had six hundred and forty acres on his farm. Uncle John Hummel. And father had just a small farm of two hundred acres. He sold the first little one before we moved there. It was six miles from town. From Lebanon. And then he bought a two hundred acre farm that was just across the road from Uncle John's farm. And it was all level prairie land. Just a nice little place. And father had never farmed before. But he learned very fast and he had ideas that he wanted to try. And he did very well. But then there came the time that the winds blew and blew and blew and those who thought they knew said that that dust would continue blowing for seven years. It blew the wheat right out of the ground. And that dust bowl formed at that time. And there were many many people in that area lost their farms after several years of that dust bowl farming. But my dad wouldn't stay for that. After the first time his wheat blew out of the ground he said, I've finished here. We're going to Oregon. And that was in nineteen nine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what town was it near in Nebraska?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Lebanon. Near Lebanon. We were four miles from Lebanon. And so he decided to pick up and go to work. And we were there for four years. We had moved from Colorado Springs to Nebraska, Lebanon, Nebraska. And we stayed there four years and father didn't stay long enough to lose his farm or to lose anything. He had money enough to come west and start over again. So he came out in nineteen nine. I think that was the summer of the Alaska Yukon Pacific Exposition in Seattle. He came out with his little Oxford bag. I don't know just how long he stayed. Perhaps two weeks. And he looked over all the country around Seattle and down in Oregon. He especially wanted to go to Oregon. And he found a place that he thought he would like to be. It was near McMinnville. And so that autumn when he came back, oh it was quite late in the autumn, he had an auction and sold everything in the house. He had sold the stock too. And he said we're going to sell everything except what goes in our suitcases and mother's trunk. And I was at school when the auction took place. And then in February, as soon as the first semester I was in, I was in my second year of high school and the first semester had ended. It was sometime in February that we came to Oregon to McMinnville.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father sell things that you really missed?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I imagine if you had to sell most everything, you must have been attached to some of your own things.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes, but I had to forget about them. There were some things I thought about for a long while afterward, but they were gone. The sale took place while I was in school. But anyway, then we stayed with Uncle John and Aunt Nettie until we were ready to leave. Yes, he sold the farm, the house and everything. And we went to McMinnville and we were there about six weeks, I think. It was in February and it rained and rained and rained and we didn't do anything else but rain. I started to school there and my sister did too. That was all the family they had. We were two girls. And I thought it was beautiful. I just loved all that rain. It had been so dry in Nebraska and the dust was blowing. And over in Oregon there were lemons on the trees, both fruit and blossoms. And I just loved walking to school in the rain. My mother said she just couldn't live there. That was all there was to it. It was too wet for her. And so, Father said, well, alright, if it isn't pleasing to you, it isn't going to be a place for us to live and so we'll go farther inland. And they came to Moscow. They knew a family there in Moscow by the name of Egland. Susie and Peter Egland. And they had a ten acre apple orchard. Rome Beauties and other kinds of apples. It was a beautiful orchard and they kept it so nice and clean because Susie would go out in the spring and she would hoe for, I think it was six or ten feet around each one of those trees and ten acres. And that was their farming, was that apple orchard. And then we had rented rooms in Moscow and I started to school again in Moscow, to the high school.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And Father started going out with real estate agents to different places around, outside of Moscow, and even came over here as far as Princeton. And he liked to farm up toward Gold Hill. So he bought 120 acres, I think, up there near Gold Hill. And I didn't see it until June. The family came over here and moved into a little up and down house that was on the place. They built a new house later. But for the time being, this was a little three room shack, is what they called it, from Mr. and Mrs. Charlie Lemon. And the Lemons moved down to Princeton and my father and mother and sister moved into the little shack up on the farm. And there were 40 acres that had to be cleared. It was all in pine trees and fir trees. And there was 40 acres cleared and that was what he was farming. And then he had my uncle to slash off another 40. And that wood was just burned up to get it out of the way, to make a place to plant crops. Of course it took time. And then the other 40, my father cleared most of that himself. That made the three 40s. And they built the house up on the hill on the third 40. They used to go up there and my mother said, this is such a beautiful spot. She said, I'd like to build our house up here. It was on top of the hill. So father said, all right, we'll build it up here. And he had a contractor to move up here. That was in 1913, three years after they had come to Princeton.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: You finished out the school year in Moscow and then moved up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The family had gone before you did or did you all go to the beginning?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh, then I went up to the Ursuline Convent.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And I went to school there for the next three years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where exactly was that then, the Ursuline Convent? Pardon? Where was it located then?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Ursuline Convent in Moscow. Oh, yes. At St. Mary's Academy now. And I was graduated from there in 1912.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And I had always wanted to teach school. And so Mother Mary Rose gave me some special credits and some tutoring for subjects that were required for teaching. And then right from the convent with those credits that Mother Rose had arranged for me, I had a scholarship. And I could have entered the university then, but I wanted to teach here first. And I went to Colfax and took the teacher's examination in the spring of 1913. And then all the schools by that time in later counties were to be taken, contracted for. So I kept writing out of the state. I even wrote to Alaska for a position. And I wrote to several other places. But one of them was accepted at Creston, Washington. So I went out there to teach that term of school. It was 13 miles from Creston and 13 miles from Wilbur out in the Scab Rock country. But that was a different experience and I enjoyed it. I taught that term at $65 a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like for you just starting to teach as your first class?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I was in the schoolhouse and I had to walk a mile down one hill and up another. And there were 14 pupils from grade one through eight.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And what I watched most for was the mail carrier who drove in from Creston. Oh, he'd get there about noon. Because I got a letter every day from Evan. And I don't think he ever missed a day sending a letter all that time I was there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When had you met Kevin?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Where I died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When had you met him?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh, in 1910, the summer of 1910, when I came home in June, my family lived up there on the farm. And his family lived down at Princeton. His father was a postmaster there. 35 years. And he had a general store. And I think it was at Sunday school that I met him.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And I thought he was very clever because when they passed around their plate he would have a nickel balanced on each one of those fingers. And he'd put it in like that. I had never seen anyone do that before.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But I think that was where I met him. And they had the Sunday school and the schoolhouse. There wasn't any church built there in this town then.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And he wrote to you every day.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Every day. I don't think he missed a day.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: He must have really liked you a lot then.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, I don't know whether he had said he did or not, but he wrote the letters anyway. They were just little letters. Just little friendly letters. They weren't romantic or amorous or anything. But I still had them tied up with the little red ribbon.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: So it sounds like a pretty isolated time to be teaching in the middle of nowhere and away from home.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: It sounds like it wouldn't be the easiest job in the world for a person to do. I didn't expect it to be easy. I went there to teach and to work. And I boarded with a very nice family who lived a mile from the schoolhouse. And three of their children went to school.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: We had programs at the school and all the people who had children would come and we would have...

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Songfests and dialogues and such things as they had at school on Friday afternoons. They used to have those at Princeton too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would there be parties at the school? Did they have parties at the school then? Like neighborhood get -togethers?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: No. The neighbors were so far apart.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: So every day was like a party at the school. There wasn't any... It wasn't any well at the schoolhouse. There was a little woodshed of sorts where they had a little wood for kindling and coal. We just had to heat the schoolhouse with a little iron stove. And I used to carry a little ten pound large bucket full of water from home. The children brought their drinking water in bottles to school with their lunch. And I brought this little bucket full of water to pour over their hands before they would eat their lunch. They would all stand in a row outside in the school yard and hold out their hands. And I'd pour a little bit of water on each one. And so they could rinse them off clean enough to eat their sandwiches. And that was all the water that we had there unless somebody hauled some to clean the schoolhouse. They did that from time to time. Otherwise I would just sweep it out and dust it. It was a new schoolhouse though. It was a nice little frame schoolhouse painted white. But I think we had a Halloween program and we had a Christmas program.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And I think that was about all the get -togethers we had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the teaching like? What did you think of the teaching there? Was it difficult to teach?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: No, of course there weren't too many in any one grade. There were three big boys in the eighth grade and their folks would keep them out to help with the farm work too much of the time. So they didn't pass and they weren't there enough really to make it count. I don't know what they did after I was there. I didn't come back the second year.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But those little girls, there were three little girls in the fourth grade. They'd come with their sunbonnets on. I took pictures of them out in the school yard. I still have all those.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I took pictures of them out in the school yard. I still have all those. And there were two in the first grade.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I think Halloween, I don't remember just what we did. We wanted to, oh we had a basket social. That was it. We wanted the money to buy more library books. We had a little school library. That's what the Halloween was for. A basket social. I don't remember how much we made but we bought quite a few books. Books were not nearly so expensive then as they are now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get a chance to come back home very often during that time?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I came home at Christmas time was all. Two weeks.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But then you didn't go back the next year.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I didn't go back. I went back the second term, yes. I mean the second year then. No, I didn't go back for the second year. When I taught the two semesters.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And when I went home for Christmas my folks had moved into their new house up on the hill. It was very nice up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what did you do then after you stopped teaching at that school?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I came home in May and I had a month to make a trousseau and got married in June. And I've been married now 62 years.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: It will be 62 years the 11th of June. Through the very same heaven.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, where were you married? Where did you have the ceremony?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: In my parents home up on the hill and Evan brought over Reverend Jonathan Watson from Moscow to perform the ceremony. He was an Episcopalian minister and we had 25 guests.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: They all, well no they weren't all from Princeton.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: There were girls from over at Moscow and Chalice played the wedding music. Chalice Thomas. She lived just across the road up on the other hill.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And she had just come home from normal school at Cheney. She went to school at Cheney. She wanted to teach pool. But I had always wanted to do school and I had planned to be an old maid. That was all I ever had wanted to do was to be a school teacher. And Evan said well go ahead and teach your school. I said I'll wait for you. And so he did and I went and taught a year at least. And by that time I thought maybe it wasn't quite so romantic to teach school after all. But I would have gone on if it hadn't been for him. I would have found a school somewhere else. But I wouldn't have gone back to Creston. It was rather bleak and not very exciting there. I never went anywhere all the time I was there. I just stayed out at that place. Boarded for twenty dollars a month. But I liked the family. They were very nice to me.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And I roomed with their daughter. We had a little room upstairs. And we had to sleep between two feather beds. So that was an experience too. Pauline was about the same age that I was.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Their name was Blens. Mr. and Mrs. John Blens and their four children.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think you got the idea to want to be a teacher?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I think in Nebraska. My mother and father always lived there. Well, when we first went to Nebraska there was a little sod schoolhouse on one corner of that farm. There was an acre taken out of that farm for a schoolyard. And there was a sod schoolhouse on it. And that summer some of the men in the school district took down that sod schoolhouse. And they built a new little white frame schoolhouse. And, oh, it was just a charming little place. It wasn't so hard to get water there. There were just a few children around the neighborhood, a few families who sent their children there. There was my cousin and then the two girls in our family. And three other families each had one child there. My mother and the Haig family had about six children who came. They had ten in their family, the Haig family. And we'd play ball at recess and we had such a good time. And my father and mother always boarded the school teacher. And I think I was in the sixth grade when we came there from Colorado Springs. And so there was the sixth and the seventh and the eighth grade. And those school teachers all boarded with my father and mother. And I admired them so much, all my school teachers. I just thought I'd like to be a school teacher too.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: So that's the way it came about, I think.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: For our eighth grade graduation, we went to the county school.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: All had their eighth grade graduation there. That was really quite an event. We went on the train from our town to the county school. We went from McCook to the county seat. And my mother had a white dress made by a dressmaker for me to wear. And all the girls wore white dresses. And the boys all wore dark suits. I have the picture somewhere tucked away of our eighth grade class from all over the county. That was really quite an event.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find moving from place to place to be difficult for you when you were a child?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I know sometimes you find it difficult when I get some friends to have to leave them.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, I often, I wouldn't say often, but I used to wish that I could live in one town all my life and know everybody and not have to make new friends everywhere I went. Yes, I did. I did notice that. But then children adjust easily. They quickly find new interests and new friends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, it seems certainly that since you settled down here, you have spent pretty much your adult life in one place, haven't you?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes, yes, this has been just idyllic. There was one friend, or there is one friend, in Colorado Springs. We went to the same Sunday school. Her name was Merdice's Hoover.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And just about a year after we moved to Nebraska from Colorado Springs, her family moved to Seattle. And we still call each other on the telephone and write letters. She graduated from high school in Seattle and then from the University, from Washington University, University of Washington, and she became a professor in the School of Arts. And she had her master's degree in five arts, and she taught there until, she was 70, I think, until retirement age.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I think in Washington, you may retire at 65, but you are retired at 70. So she stayed the full time. Her husband died after they had their 50th anniversary. She had married Perry Hensley just after they were out of high school. And I used to go over to visit them. I went to visit them once while I was at Creston. That was a happy break that I had while I was over there. At Thanksgiving time, I went to Cooley City where she and Perry had, they had built themselves a home on his father's ranch. He wanted to have a ranch for each of his boys. And they were to have all the increase of the stock. It was a cattle ranch up in Moses Cooley. I think maybe it's underwater now. Well, no, it isn't either, because she has been back there since. And there's nothing left of the house or the barn or anything. But I went and stayed with them that weekend of Thanksgiving. I went on the train. And that was the only break I had during that term of teaching.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Probably quite a few of the homesteaders around Creston, I mean quite a few of the people there were probably homesteaders, weren't they?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: They were some of the earlier settlers in that area, I would guess.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: They may have been. I don't know about that. But they were right at the edge of the scab rot country. They had, they cut hay along the creek bottoms. And I think they had a little crop blend, but not very much. They didn't have very much grain. It was rocky country, you know that part of Washington is. It's just beyond the wheat country. They raised turkeys and they raised stock. And it seems as if down in that valley along the creek bottom they had enough hay for their stock. Good.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I'm curious about your experience at the Ursuline Academy. I don't know of other people who went there at the same time that you did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At Ursuline Academy? Yes, what was it like?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: It was a girls' boarding school. And they had girls from kindergarten age on through high school. The little kindergarten people didn't board there when I was there. They were just high school girls. But there were three nuns who operated that school. There was Mother Mary Rose and Sister Carmel and Mother Paula. She was the music teacher. And they had the day school also for...

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: It was a parochial school, mostly Catholics, but I wasn't a Catholic. But I had gone to a Catholic academy for my first year in high school. I went from Nebraska back to Nauvoo, Illinois to St. Mary's Academy. Because that was where my mother had grown up in Nauvoo and she liked that school and she wanted me to go there. And this was very similar, but it was a smaller school, of course. The one at Moscow had... I don't remember just how many, but they had all the grades. And they had the three nuns taught school and they had other teachers besides.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much religious instruction as part of the school?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Not any for me.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: You weren't required to take the religious instruction if you weren't a Catholic and didn't care for it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the studies difficult?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: They were regular high school subjects, all of them. Because it was an accredited high school and we had our certificates to enter the university when we graduated. I still have mine, I didn't use it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of the boarding part of it? What was it like to be living with other young ladies away from home?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh, it was delightful. Yes, it was delightful. It was just the same day after day. We had to get up on time in the morning and go to chapel. The chapel was just downstairs. And Father Pakul would come over from the parsonage and have his service at the chapel every morning and prayers for the nuns. And then we would go to our classrooms. We had breakfast downstairs. And we always had apple sauce and oatmeal and all the bread and butter we wanted. But that was our staple breakfast and we all were nice and plump and pink -cheaped from a diet like that. It was always the same but it was always good. It was very good. And we never did get tired of it. And then for our noon meal we had other things. I don't remember just what. Bean soup and beef stew maybe. The only thing I remember especially was the carrots. I didn't care for carrots and I guess none of the other girls did either. But we didn't eat our creamed carrots and someone told Sister Paula we didn't. And she said, well, they'll just keep coming back to the table until they do eat them.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: So I suppose they disappeared. And then for supper we had what Dolly Bean used to call glorious potatoes.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: She'd say, oh, aren't these potatoes glorious?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And they were home grown nice Moscow farm potatoes, you know. They were cooked with their jackets on I think and then chopped up and fried in butter. Lots of butter. And we had apple sauce with those and bread and butter. And not very many desserts that I can remember. But boarding for $20 a month we couldn't have very many desserts for that many girls, you know. But it was pleasing anyway. We never found a bit of fault with anything. The only thing that was uncomfortable I would say about it was our cold feet. We had little cots that we slept on in the dormitory. And every night we put our bricks on top of the cooking stove down in the kitchen and get those bricks warm and wrap them up in towels and put them in our cots to warm our feet. And one night Elizabeth Wiley's brick was too hot. And it scorched her mattress and we could smell something burning. We called Sister Carmel and she made an examination of all those cots till she found that hot brick that was scorching. And we took it out and then we all settled down.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But it was very comfortable. We had a good time there.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: It sounds like there was quite a bit of companionship among the girls.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh yes, there was. We were just like sisters. But there were girls from Genesee and girls from Moscow. And there were two of us from over at Princeton. Mary Comerford was going over there at that time. And there were Moscow girls that came for day school. There were lots of those.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the girls that attended from the county, from this county area, or were there quite a few who came from outside as well?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I don't think there were any from outside the county boarding. Not that I remember right now. But I do remember there were several from Genesee.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get a chance to go downtown to see Moscow?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes, we were permitted to go downtown shopping. There were always two of us together. And we had to report when we were going and when we returned. We had to have permission. We didn't just run whenever we felt like it. We had to have permission.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember much of what Moscow town was like at that time?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, I remember that last year I was there, they built that post office that is so much a controversial subject now. They were building that post office then. And Evan was going to the university, but I didn't know him then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the sisters very strict in their teaching and discipline?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes. Oh dear, yes. I should say they were.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say the girls were afraid of them, of stepping out of line and of being chastised?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I don't know of anybody that ever was chastised or anything. They knew the rules and they abided by them. I don't know what would have happened if they didn't. I never broke one to find out. But we had our study period after supper. We'd all go up to our study room and get our lessons for the next day. And we had such a nice big green yard and there was a big apple tree. It had the nicest big golden apples on it that fell down and got covered with snow. And even in February we'd go out there and scrape off the snow and get those apples and they were still good. I don't know if that was just one year that that happened like that or not. And then Sister Carmel raised a cucumber bed. She had a little garden, she raised cucumbers. We liked to help her in her little garden. The sisters seemed to do all their own work. They had a woman come in to do the laundering for themselves and the girls. The girls could launder their own things if they wanted to. But there were no washing machines. One did it by hand. It was all done by hand.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And then the sisters' recreation seemed to be taking a case knife and digging dandelions out of the lawn. So we used to help Mother Rose do that. They had a large lawn. It was some large house that had been made into this school. And they built a chapel onto it. There are all new buildings there now since I have come away. See, that's been so long ago. 1912 was when I finished there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you remember what any of the rules were that the girls abided by?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I'm curious to know what the kinds of rules were that it was expected that everyone obeyed.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: You know, you were saying before that no one ever thought of breaking the rules and I was just wondering what some of those rules were.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh, well, we just had to be ourselves, I guess.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I don't remember any particular rules. We just had to be good girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go home and visit very much during that time?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: No, only at Christmas.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Because I had to come on the train. We had to change trains at Palouse to the WINM to come up here. And Thanksgiving, we didn't have Friday after Thanksgiving. That wasn't a holiday. We went to school.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I don't know why. I didn't come home more often. I guess it just wasn't handy for so short a time.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: At Christmas, we had two weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember how Christmas was celebrated around here at that time when you were growing up? Was there much of a community celebration or was it just each family in its own way?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: There was always a Christmas tree at the church and a Christmas program and treats for everybody from as far back as I can remember. At all the different places we lived, it was always the same.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: They still have those Christmas programs, Christmas music, and everybody taking part.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But at the convent, they had recitals from time to time for the music pupils. And they had to play once a year. And all of us would take part in those. Just as a part of the requirements for school, I guess, for graduating.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: We didn't have any chemistry class. There was no laboratory at the school. You had to wait for college to have anything like that.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: We had four years of Latin at the convent and two years of French and four years of English, which is something that they're talking about now. They feel that the high school students aren't getting enough instruction in English now because when they go to college they don't seem to be prepared.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you first start going to Sunday school here? To Sunday school here?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh, when we first came, we went to Sunday school in the schoolhouse. There was no church here yet then. And that summer they did build a large church over where the little clubhouse is now. Just taking up donations and the people around all the community. They called it a union church because there was no particular denomination here with enough people for a church. So they were building a union church. And that first summer after they built it there were 100 Sunday school pupils attending there. Some of them were Methodists and Episcopalians. They might have been Baptists. They just all went to have Bible study and they had some good teachers who had been college teachers before they came to Princeton. And some good musicians and some good singers. Those people who were here then seemed to bring all that ability with them. So it really was a thriving Sunday school. And they would have ministers from over at Moscow and there was one traveling minister who came frequently. He was Reverend Farrell. They called him the Logger's Preacher. Dick Farrell. Dick Farrell, yes. He used to stop here with Evan's father and mother. Whenever he came to Princeton he would stay with them. And there would always be a large turnout for his sermons. And there was a Reverend Kincaid who came from Palouse frequently.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a judge too? Was he a judge too or was he related?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: There was a Judge Kincaid from Palouse that I remember reading about. A man named Kincaid who was a judge I think.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, possibly. I wouldn't know about that really. And then there was an Episcopalian minister. I think his name was Mitchell who came over frequently too. His family used to come over for weekends and they would stop with Evan's father and mother. And Evan's folks were Episcopalian. And my folks were Methodist.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But it didn't matter what you were. You were welcome to the Union Church. And it went on for a number of years. And then the women formed Union Sunday School Society. And whenever the church needed repairs they would have bazaars and dinners. And they did quilting as they're still doing. And they would furnish the materials and then the men in the community would do the work. And they put a new roof at one time on that big church. I don't know if anyone has any pictures of that church. I don't have one. But somebody might have. It was a large building. And it was sealed inside. It wasn't plastered. It was sealed. I wish I had taken some pictures of it. But I never did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, on most Sundays was there usually a minister speaking at the church on a Sunday?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: There was always Sunday school on Sunday. I don't know whether they always had preaching afterwards or not. But there would be a lay preacher very often.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The service would follow the Sunday school?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: That would follow Sunday school. And then from time to time the young people would all get into buggies and hacks and go down to poplats to Christian Endeavor Society on Sunday evenings.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I remember doing that from time to time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And there would be a lay preacher there?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, it was supported by the union church at Poplats. They had a large church there at one time. It was right down on the main road. It was a beautiful big building with stained glass windows. And then it burned down about 19... it must have been about 1956 or somewhere along there. In the 50s.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But this Christian Endeavor Society was for young people?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A study group?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Uh huh. Someone would have a message like a sermonette. It was usually an evening affair. We'd go down before dark and get back before dark. It wasn't late. But I think they had it every week in Potlatch but we didn't always go down. I guess we didn't always have someone to take us.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: People who had horses and buggies and two -seaters liked to go places on Sundays. But when we could we had someone to take us down from this Sunday school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Dick Farrell's preaching?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh yes, I went off into his services.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I wonder what his services were like. I've heard so much about him preaching to the lumberjacks in the logging camps.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, his sermons were just like any other preacher's but he had a way of delivering that they liked to hear him talk. They liked to listen. I couldn't tell you what any one sermon was but...

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I wonder what made him so popular. He seemed to be someone that many people liked and admired.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes. Well, he just had a good way about him. He was just a good person.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I think he was a man of God. I think he really was through and through.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: When he came here to stay he would get right busy and fill up grandmother's wood box just as if he were one of the family and I imagine he did the same whenever he went. And I remember one time I was going to go to Boise on the train. I had a new granddaughter and I was going down to stay for a couple of weeks. And the snow was so deep and it was so cold and I had to have someone take me down to Palouse to catch the train to go to Spokane. And the train was late. But when I got on board, Reverend Farrell was on. And he said, the train is late. And he said, when we get into Spokane, he said there will be a train waiting to go to Boise and he said you won't have very much time. So he said, I'll take your suitcases. I had two of them. And he said, I'll take those and run on ahead. And you just get there as fast as you can over to the other train. So he got off first and he telephoned over to the other railroad station. You had to go to another railroad station to get that train. And he said to wait because there was a passenger coming on that train from Palouse that wanted to go to Boise.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So I got there as fast as I could and when I got there, the train was waiting and the conductor was standing outside and he said, are you the lady that's going to Boise?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And I said yes. And so he said, well, everything is on so you just get on. If it hadn't been for Reverend Farrell, I never would have made it. But he was just so helpful wherever he went, you know.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I think wherever there was a church and they asked him to come. As far as I know, they just invited him and if he was free for that Sunday, he would come. He went to all the lumber camps to preach. Bobo.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I think he came for over a period of several years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Sunday school, was it divided into classes with different teachers for the different age groups?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes, there were different teachers for different age groups.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: B .F. Thomas was the superintendent for a long time. H .L. Hawkins was the superintendent before Mr. Thomas. And Mrs. Henry Allen was our teacher and she was a wonderful teacher. She had taught in Oberlin College before she came here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was her husband working or farming here as well?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: No. I don't know what her husband did. I think he was retired.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I think he may have done some carpenter work. It was Henry Allen and she had her son Forrest Allen who was a photographer. And her daughter Fern had been a teacher before she came here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a social part of the Sunday school as well? Was there an opportunity for young people to meet and talk and mix with each other?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Or was it a strictly religious... They would all make something to take for a picnic lunch and go up to the spring on Gold Hill. That's the only picnics I ever attended.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This would be after the church service was over?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes, after Sunday school. While they went to Sunday school at the schoolhouse, I don't remember attending any sermons until after they built the church. But that was the first summer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the church service mostly or entirely just the sermon or was there a worship and singing and prayers as well?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Just a real service such as any church would have.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But since it was a united church, it wouldn't be a sectarian service. I'm wondering how it might be different than an episcopal service or a presbyterian service or a Methodist service.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, I don't know. They just made a few arrangements. The Episcopalian's had a screen made that they would set up for. I never happened to come up here for one of those services. We lived on the way then. Oh. Evan and I lived on the way. We didn't always live here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was Evan's parents' store like here? What was their store like?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: A general store. And the post office was in the back of it until they built a separate addition for the post office. And they had coffee that you ground in a big coffee mill. Coffee didn't come in cans and jars just then. It came in paper bags and you could have it ground or you could take it home and grind it in a little hand grinder. Their coffee mill is still upstairs. A nice big coffee mill.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And they had oranges by the dozen and bananas by the dozen. I don't think they weighed the bananas. They were cut off the stem and clotted.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And they had some dry goods and canned goods. Just like any little country store but not a supermarket. I think all that my mother ever sent for down to the store was coffee and sugar and canned salmon and bacon. She had everything else on the farm.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And so did we have in Nebraska.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: You cooked everything from scratch in those days. And recipes were called receipts. There's a new receipt book out now from Mrs. Pratt in Cordial Lane. It's the nicest little book and it's called Patience Willowleaf's Receipt Book. And it's all about the things that the pioneers had to cook with when they came in their covered wagons. It's very interesting. I just finished reading it. She sent me a copy of it. And she has done hours and hours of research getting that little cookbook together. And she has it for sale now. Grace Raffy Pratt of Cordial Lane.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your mother and did you cook in a way that was similar to the kinds of recipes described in that book?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Very much, mhm. But of course my mother had magazines and recipes that came at that time. It was a little bit later than the covered wagons.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I wondered if it had changed very much from the pioneer days in cooking.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, it has changed now because everything is already prepared. The cake mixes are prepared and all of those things. But the other recipes are just about the same as what my mother had. I've been reading them and I think they're very similar. Only one thing I noticed in particular, the pioneers didn't seem to have double -acting baking powder. They would use two teaspoons of cream of tartar and one of soda for their leavening in their cookies and cakes.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And now it's just a teaspoon of double -acting baking powder to a cup of flour, mostly, unless you buy a mix.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, with people not buying too much food the way they do nowadays, did Evan's parents do a good business at their general store just the same?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh, yes. People bought something. Everybody didn't live out on the farm, you know, and have all those things. They would raise all those things. But they would have their own stock if they lived on the farm and do their own butchering. And made their own butter and their cottage cheese. They even made yellow cheese. And, of course, they had their own bacon and hams. They smoked their own hams.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I know I've been told by one or two former storekeepers that a lot of times they had to carry people on credit maybe for a pretty long time. Like the farmers would have to carry them over until the harvest. Until the crops were so old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that true here too with Evan's parents? That they had to carry them?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Of course they carried some. I really don't know about that. But I know it was true in Onaway where Evan and his brother had the store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, that store in Onaway, was that a grocery store too? A general store?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes, it was a general store. It was used now for a church.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: The last man who had a store there moved over to Potlatch into that new shopping center. And then the building was sold for a church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Didn't a lot of people from Potlatch do their shopping in Onaway rather than some of them didn't want to shop at the Mercantile's store?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh yes, they did a great deal of merchandising with people in Potlatch. They would even send a man over to take orders in the morning, go from house to house. That was a convenience to the housewives at one time. And then deliver them that afternoon. And then they began phoning in their orders and they would deliver those. They had a team of horses and a wagon they delivered. That is before they had a truck or a pickup.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know how your prices compared to the prices at the Potlatch Mercantile store?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: They were about the same I think.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I've heard that was a very big store, the Merc.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But of course the farmers liked to come into Onaway. Most of the farmers came to bring their butter and eggs.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: They seemed to do well enough and kept busy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much of a town at Onaway at that time besides your store?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: No, it was just about the same as it is now. I notice many of the houses are just the same ones that were there when we lived there. And we left there in 1938. And I don't see that it's very much changed but people are fixing up their places more because they have water. They have a water system now and a sewage system. They've incorporated. And it wasn't incorporated when we were there. It's shaping up. It looks much nicer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you lived there from when you were married until 1938?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well we went to Spokane for a year at one time and then for four months at another time. And all the rest of the time we lived there in Onaway until we moved up here. Moved up to our 49 acres up there in 1938. On decoration day. And then we moved down here on the 9th of November in 1948. After Eben's father and mother had died the last day of February and the first day of March.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The day, one day between them?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: There were just 12 hours between them, their passing. And that was when the high water came here. They had to bring an army truck instead of an ambulance to take Grandpa out.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Grandmother died in the evening about 9 o 'clock and Grandpa died in the next morning about 9 o 'clock. I wasn't here then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think her death caused his? Do you think that her passing caused his death?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: No, I don't think he knew it. No,

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I don't think either one of them knew how near the other was to passing. They had both been ill for some time. Their daughter and their granddaughter and other people were here but I wasn't here.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, I have heard almost... I think she was 86 or 87.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I have heard almost nothing about the little town of Onaway back in the 20s and 30s. It's a place and a county that I know almost nothing at all about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About Onaway?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I think it was a potlatch and people did go from potlatch there to there to shop but there are so few old timers from Onaway from those days that are around.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But it was really a little town of its own. There's something in here about Onaway I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Onaway?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I haven't come to it yet. I don't know just what it is.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But it was its own little town, a little separate town of its own even back then.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: It never had a post office but there was a schoolhouse there that was school district 101.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And they had a dentist office and they had a meat market and they had a drug store. And Dr. Lippard had a little homelike hospital. He called it the North Potlatch Sanatorium I think. But they had rooms upstairs for patients.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Dr. Lippard had been a ship doctor years before and his wife was a nurse. She was the first licensed pharmacist in Idaho, Mrs. Lippard.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: They had a lovely drug store.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: The shelves were just lined with those big apothecaries jars like those up there.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh they had just dozens and dozens of those filled with things on their shelves and they were always kept shining. He had a Japanese boy who came over and kept everything just immaculate in that drug store. And Mrs. Lippard made and sold hats in the room back of the drug store. She had a millinery shop and that was something you don't see anymore as a millinery shop. But she had worked in a store at Garfield before she came on the way, before she married Dr. Lippard. And she used to even line coffins that they had made. They had carpenters make the coffins and she would line them with satin and pillows and ruffles. She did that in her millinery shop in Garfield. She had one in Garfield before she came to Onaway. She didn't do any of that work after she came to Onaway, only the hats. But she'd have the most beautiful hats with ribbons and flowers. That's the days of Easter bonnets. We don't see so many of those anymore. But they were beautiful creations.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she sell clothing as well? Dresses? Did she sell dresses as well?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: No, she had only hats.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: The hats and the nursing kept her busy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work in the grocery store too, in Onaway?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes, some of the time after my children were in school. I didn't work while my children were at home. But after they were all in school I did mostly bookkeeping and waiting on customers. They didn't wait on themselves then like they do now. The Piggly Wiggly stores started that. While we were in Spokane they started the Piggly Wiggly stores. Go around and help yourself and check out. But in our store we helped the customers find what they wanted.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Stood there and waited while they decided and then if they didn't want anything, why?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: That time had just gone by, but that's the way it was. And then you cut off the cheese instead of every slice being wrapped up carefully in plastic the way it is now. There was a big cake of cheese that, oh dear, it was probably 14 inches across or larger. And one heavy knife that cut off a pound at a time. You whirled it around on a little lazy Susan, like a lazy Susan, and cut it off. Sometimes you'd get a pound and sometimes you wouldn't. It had to be weighed. And then we had to dip the sugar out of one big sack and put it into little paper bags. It didn't come in packages the way it does now. And we got so we could lift out a pound of nails out of a barrel full and put them in a paper sack. We got so we could just pick up a pound and know just about to the nail if it weighed a pound or two pounds. That was different than it is now. And now there's so much packaging.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: One Englishman came over from London and someone asked him about his impressions of America. And he said, I haven't had time to make any impressions. He said, I've been so busy unwrapping cellophane packages.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: He didn't know just what he thought of it all yet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of your business come from farmers around on the way? Is that where the main source was? Did many people from Potlatch use the store?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I wouldn't know how many but enough to keep everybody busy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many people ask for credit in those days or need credit?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I know people weren't too well to do back then. No.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, I don't remember how many of course. It would be hard to say.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But I know after the depression a lot of them never were able to pay up. That store lost out entirely during the depression. The merchants association had to take the accounts and whatever they got out of them I don't know. But I know that we lost considerably.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Everything in fact. I would imagine that no one would have had any money. Because money doesn't mean very much to Evan. And as long as he had anything he let people have it.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And there was quite a bit on the books when we had to close up.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Because we couldn't get any more credit. And therefore we couldn't give any more. So that was just the end of it. Some of the people came and paid afterwards just because they knew that they owed it. But there were many others who never could.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And whether the merchants association got anything from some of them or not I don't know. But they probably got enough to satisfy their creditors.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you close the store? Was that in 1938 or was that earlier?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I think that was about the time it was.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I really would have to look up something to find out about that.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I was just curious.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Because that didn't impress me too much.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I was just curious about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find it to be a full time job to raise your children and keep the house going?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Never had any time to spare. But of course in those days we didn't have all the gadgets for housekeeping that we do now. We didn't have electricity and on our way. We lighted gasoline lamps. Everyone had a gasoline lamp or lantern or something or coal oil lamps. Prior to 1938 we didn't have electricity and on our way. I think the REA came in 1938. When we moved up there we had electricity in our house up here. And that was when they put it in. But they had it down here in Princeton before that.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And of course then we didn't have vacuum cleaners. And instead of refrigerators, we had Leonard ice boxes. And we put 50 pound cake of ice in our ice box. And that kept the milk from turning sour. That was about all.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: You put a big pan under the ice box and that caught the dripping water. Before your 50 pound cake was gone you put in another one. And that meant that you had to have ice houses all around the country. My father had an ice house up at his place. Evan's father had one here. Everyone had to have an ice house and fill it up in the winter. Cut the cakes out of the river and bury them in sawdust.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean most farms had their own ice houses?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes, if they wanted any ice they did.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And I think most of them did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the ice house underground?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: No, it was over. But it had to be thick walls filled with sawdust. Double walls filled with sawdust. And they buried the ice cakes in sawdust.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And they had part, would they do that as a group effort to go out and cut ice on the river? Or where would they get their ice from?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: They cut it from the river as I remember. I don't know where else they would get it. We used to have more severe winters than we have had lately. It would be really cold below zero for a long time. We had one night this winter of zero weather. It was all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think you raised your children the way that you had been raised by your parents?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, pretty much so. I wouldn't know how else to raise them.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I wonder if it's much different than the way children are growing up now, nowadays.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, they get different ideas when they start to college. I think until they started to college they were just the way we had always taught them to be. And expected them to be. But even college now is vastly different from when my children went.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: We had three boys.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: The first boy died in 1945. He didn't go to college. He was taking some correspondence courses from the university and he did some newspapers.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: In 1944, one of them graduated in 1944 and then he went into the Navy. And the other one had already gone into the Army and he came back and graduated in 1947. He came back and finished.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh, that's Geeta. She's another old -timer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Oh, she knows some good stories. And then, after that, under that pine tree, Evan's father and the boys and some other neighbors who helped had a form. And they mixed their cement right there and made the blocks in the form. And they loaded them all onto a flat wagon with low wheels and hauled them down to the river and put them in the water to harden. The river at that time didn't have steep banks the way it does now. We could just wade across. It was just a pretty little river with clear water. And then they would haul those cement blocks back and made enough of them. Evan's father told me how many are in this house, but I forget. I was thinking it was stone. I may have written it down somewhere, but I never bothered to count them.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And they have blocks. I was thinking it was rock. And they're hollow inside like those that you buy now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year was it that this house was built?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: 1910. They built it in 1910. That summer. Herman and I and Neil and I were talking about it this afternoon and we wondered. Nobody seemed to know when it was built.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: 1910. Herman wasn't sure whether George built it or George and folks or something. George and Sarah. Sarah, that was her name. Mm -hmm. And there are walls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's one of the few houses around here that's built from out of those cement blocks, isn't it?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I mean, it's not too usual to see. Well, I think this is the only one that was ever handmade right here on the ground. Of course, those trees weren't so tall then.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I wonder where that cement came from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The cement?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, Portland, I think.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I was going to ask you if you remember the gypsies coming through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The gypsies?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Yes, I remember when the gypsies used to come. They were just, oh, they were so picturesque. They had full flowing skirts of bright colors, you know. It looked as if they had more than one on. It looked as if they might have had half a dozen petticoats. And they'd walk up the railroad track. And I followed them one day with the Kodak. I wanted to get pictures of them because I thought they were so picturesque. There was a woman and some smaller girls. I don't know where they were going, but they were going up the track. And they didn't want me to take their picture. And I said, oh, let me take your picture. You're so pretty. And they kept on running. I started running too, and I snapped it. I said, well, I got it anyway.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And she said, you got it. You didn't get my face. You just got my behind.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: We were living up there at the Forks. We turned to go to Fernwood and then on to St. Mary's. You know, that Forks one summer. Oh, in 39 or 40, long in there. And there was two big camps of gypsies up there. Oh, there were. And I was afraid of them. Oh, I wasn't afraid of them. There weren't enough of them at that time, I guess, in that group. The big camps, you know, where they have all the wagons and the horses and the goats and the sheep and the cows. And they would sleep all day. And then they'd sing and dance and play around all night.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, would they?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Around Kent. Well, Emma told about their rushing the stores and filling their pockets. I don't know about that. I didn't. Yeah, well that's what I was afraid of because a lady that lived up there, they had a house, you know, she made sure everything was locked up good and nothing was laid loose out in the yard or anything. Because she said, she wasn't going to buy and they'd make her breakfast and all that. That must have been Parker's store that Emma talked about because I never heard Grandpa Grunzey say anything about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever wind up speaking to them or having anything to do with them actually?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, yes, I did have to because in order to keep on the good side I was afraid not to, you know. Because they were very friendly type of people. Very friendly. And there was one central water well up there at that time. And I had two big five gallon cans that I'd put in the trunk of my car and go up and fill them up once a day. And they were always there getting water too. In fact, one of the camps was right close to the wells. And it was up over a hill, you know, up in the open. And so there was always some of them about when I went to get my water to drink, you know, for cooking. I got down to that little creek that ran along there along the road to wash and stuff like that. But I had to go get my drinking water up there on the hill. There was always some of them around, watering their stock or something. They'd bring them down, in fact, in front of where our trailer was to water their stock. Because it was low and easy to get to. So, you know, you just don't threaten them or run and hide or anything because then they will cause you trouble. But I never let them get close to me or anything like that, you know. Kept my car doors locked and my trailer locked and that type of thing. Because they could just always slip out so easy. In the stores in Portland, one section of Portland is just over -ridden. I mean, it's just strictly a gypsy area. And they come there and come home and stay during the winter months, you know. I don't know where they go in the summertime when it gets hot. They must go north or something. But they'll be three or four and they'll start a fight in the grocery store among themselves. You know, just an off -fight. Well, you can't help but stop and watch them. In the meantime, here scoots out the back door a guy with four or five carts full of groceries. And he gets away real quick while everybody's watching this big fracas in front of them. They're slick. So when they got it down really to a fine art. Yeah, and they still do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They haven't changed, eh?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I don't know if that carry -out guy told me how much they lost in a year's time through this commotion. So most stores would post guards. But still, even then, I mean, you have a person that was like a customer wandering around shopping, you know. In fact, a friend of mine was a guard in a store. She spent half a day filling a basket and then the other half putting it back where she got it. But in the meantime, keeping an eye. And when a big fight or fracas would start, then they would start looking elsewhere for something else that was going on. But still, they'd get away with it.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But, you know, it's very colorful and they're very... They stand out more than they used to because it wasn't uncommon to have a long dress back then, you know.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, I suppose that I should really be getting going. I've been here for quite a while. We've talked about quite a few things.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And, uh... Tomorrow is the day we bring antiques to the... Some place where the old Indian didn't want them to be or something like this.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was a family?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: And the family was sort of arrogant. And I guess Grandpa Tribble really saved their lives. And he brought them into his camp. Well, the Indians respected him so much that if someone was his friend, the Indians wouldn't hurt them, wouldn't touch them, you know. Because he was breaking Grandpa Tribble that much. And so when anybody was in trouble, they would come to him and stay around him and then they weren't in danger. But the minute they'd get out of sight, you see, well then...

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: At this point, the Indians were camping all around here. This was their home ground, you see.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Because potlatch means feast grounds. And that was their feast grounds where Indians came from all over there in the summertime to carry out meetings and count on them. Beasting, fasting, and whatever was there in that area. And that was during his time.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But I just wish I could... Now, there was one hair -raising story that I can't quite put my finger on it yet. I can remember him telling some weird stories that really were unbelievable. You know, close calls of people that he got out of a tight spot. He put his own life on the line a few times to get the feast. I like this family that wanted to settle somewhere up the road, up the way a little bit, up the river. They were very arrogant and before he got them out of, he almost lost his own life in the process, you see. And from then on, he was a little more careful. And, you know, how he... But of course, this didn't last much longer. It was almost a thing of the past and the time he came, but he did settle a lot of disputes between the white man and Indians.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, it sounds like you know more about it than possibly anybody else does now. Well, I can remember him telling this kid's stories.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: You know, my folks in the Tribbles and the LaRouges, we were the only ones on the Creek at that time. And they'd all get together on Thanksgiving and whenever possible. That's the only place there was to go and the only thing there was to do, you see. Well, he was an old fellow, real old then, and a hard friend to get around. He wasn't too well. He would sit and tell us kids' stories while the rest of them was off in another room yacking her pillion cards, you see. And this is where... But if I was so young that maybe seven, eight years old, you see, six or seven or eight, well... A child that young, you get, oh, you visualize this big picture but not the facts, you know. And you just don't remember. Now, I was a teenager, though, when he passed away. I've often wished that after I'd gotten a little older that I wrote some of it down when I still had it in my mind. Because it was... Now, Lola could tell you more, I would think, than Herschel.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: May be. And I haven't asked her too much about them, about his stories. Herschel remembers his financial reverses, you know, and the land he bought and then the 93 Depression and how he lost that.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: But, you see, Herschel's folks was separated when he was quite young. Well, he lived with his mother, so he... That's why he doesn't know very much about his... And up until later years after he was married to Lola, he was quite a span in there. He lost track of his life.

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: In this period of time. That's right. He told me about that. That is the reason, as I recall...

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Stayed in town during the week. But I had an old all that would come with me when I'd come in Monday morning and he'd be there waiting for me Friday night when I got out of school. Mm -hmm. That was the railroad track run up there then. And I'd walk down the tracks. The railroad track went up in 1906. I remember that. And they had, at Bovill, had a great picnic 50 years later to celebrate the laying of that railroad track.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That went up the creek, you mean?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: That went up to Bovill. From Palouse to Bovill. And they had, they got together 11 passenger cars full of people from on down and went up to Bovill for that picnic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When was this?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: Well, it had to be in 1956 because it was a 50 year celebration. 50 year picnic.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you stay in Princeton?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: I stayed with the Ackermanns. Mary and Glen Ackermann. I already knew them that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have any girls then?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: No, they hadn't. Their children weren't born yet. Oh, uh -huh. I remember, they had one of these phone graphs with the cylinder records, you know. The ground records would just go over a disk and had a funny looking noodle deal that you put on different than what they had on the flat records. And she had a record of jingle bells. And I played every time, every night when I come home from school I'd run the first thing to put a jingle bell on the phone. One night I come home from school and I couldn't find it. No place. Well, Mary said, well you just played it so much it melted away. And I'm sure that she hid that. She was getting tired of listening to us.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened? Was that the first one on the ground that you ever really had the chance to play with?

speaker: Viola Sheldon Guernsey
words: No, my folks, the mother had one. But she had the wind -up handle, you know, on the flat record. But Mary and Angie Glen's was the round cylinder one. That's the first experience I had with that. Huh. Yeah. Well, I'm going to get going on that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will these stories keep getting?
